coredata.dc:title: One-step, green, and economic synthesis of water-soluble photoluminescent carbon dots by hydrothermal treatment of wheat straw, and their bio-applications in labeling, imaging, and sensing
coredata.prism:copyright: Copyright © 2015 Elsevier B.V. All rights reserved.
coredata.dc:creator: Yuan, Ming
Zhong, Ruibo
Gao, Haiyang
Li, Wanrong
Yun, Xiaoling
Liu, Jingran
Zhao, Xinmin
Zhao, Guofen
Zhang, Feng
coredata.dc:description: The use of biomass as renewable and sustainable energy source has attracted the attention of politics and research and development (R&D) facilities around the world. Agricultural straw acts as a typical biowaste, which still needs highly effective recycling to save the biomass urgently at present. Photoluminescent carbon dots (C-dots) are novel biocompatible nanomaterials that have been proved to be produced from many carbon-abundant materials and hold great promise for the modern nanobiomedicine. In order to realize a “one-stone-two-birds” strategy, we report a green, economic, one-pot method in this article for synthesizing photoluminescent C-dots by hydrothermal treatment of wheat straw. Using X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) and X-ray diffraction (XRD), we show that the as-prepared C-dots are amorphous in structure and are mainly composed of carbon. Their tiny size (<2nm), combined with the characteristic excitation-dependent relatively bright emission, and robust photostability made the C-dots a potential biocompatible nanomaterial for bio-applications. We have experimentally demonstrated their potential applications in biomedical labeling, imaging, and sensing/detecting. The high yield (∼20%) of C-dots from wheat straw may suggest a new economic strategy for recycling biowaste.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon dot
Photoluminescence
Straw
Labeling
Sensing
Imaging

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Labeling CaCO3 particles and bacteria for imaging

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (A) Hydrodynamic diameter (D h) distribution histogram of C-dots by DLS. The histogram statistics show that the average D h is 1.7±0.23nm with a PDI of 0.422. (B) The zeta-potential result of C-dots is −31.2mV. (C) The AFM height mode image of C-dots obtained in air, the upper and bottom bars indicate the dots’ height and scale, respectively. (D) The AFM cross-sectional analysis of (C). The insert shows the cutting line (black) and two red marks for measuring the height of one of the C-dots. (For interpretation of the references to color in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of the article.)

(A) UV–vis absorption (black line) peaks at 247 and 334nm for the C-dots, and the narrow and symmetrical PL (red line) peak at 467nm upon the excitation wavelength of 360nm of the as-prepared C-dots. The two structures in the inset are digital images of C-dots dispersed in pure water under illumination of daylight (left) and 365-nm UV light (right), respectively. (B) PL spectra of the C-dots at various excitation wavelengths from 320 to 500nm (slit=5nm). The inset is the normalized PL emission spectra. The optical images of characters written by our C-dots as ink on the UV-nonresistant paper are shown in upon illumination of (C) white light and (D) UV light. (For interpretation of the references to color in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of the article.)
The EEM (A) and 3D PL plot (B) of the as-prepared C-dots in aqueous solutions at room temperature.
(A) XPS, (B) C1s, (C) N1s, and (D) O1s spectra of the as-prepared C-dots.

Effect of (A) ionic strength and (B) pH on the photoluminescence intensity of C-dots at 450nm, when excited at 360nm. A 10-mM phosphate buffer was used for solutions with pH 2–12. All the values are the average of triplicate measurements. The stability of the fluorescence intensity of C-dots for various times under (C) white light and (D) UV light.

Imaging C-dot-labeled LBL capsules with CaCO3 template by LSCM. (A and B) LSCM images upon excitation with 488 and 568nm, respectively. (C) A bright-field differential interference contrast (DIC) image and (D) an overlay chart of (A–C).
Imaging C-dot-labeled E. coli by LSCM. (A and B) LSCM images upon excitation with 405 and 488nm, respectively. (C) A bright-field (DIC) image and (D) an overlay chart of (A–C).
Imaging C-dot-labeled C. elegans by LSCM. (A and B) LSCM images upon excitation with 405 and 488nm, respectively. (C) A bright-field (DIC) image and (D) an overlay chart of (A–C).

Metal ionic-sensing assay. (A) The comparison of C-dot sensing of different metal ions. (B) The PL spectra of C-dots with various concentrations of Fe3+, as indicated by different colored lines. (C) The Stern–Volmer plot of the PL intensity of C-dots against the concentration of the quencher Fe3+. F and F 0 represent the PL intensities with and without quenchers, respectively. (For interpretation of the references to color in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of the article.)

head.ce:title: One-step, green, and economic synthesis of water-soluble photoluminescent carbon dots by hydrothermal treatment of wheat straw, and their bio-applications in labeling, imaging, and sensing

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Agricultural Nanocenter, School of Life Sciences, Inner Mongolia Agricultural University, 306 Zhaowuda Road, Hohhot 010018, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Agricultural Nanocenter, School of Life Sciences, Inner Mongolia Agricultural University

ce:footnote.ce:note-para: These authors contributed equally to this work.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Water-soluble photoluminescent carbon dots can be synthesized simply by a green, economic and one-pot hydrothermal treatment of wheat straw with ∼20% yield, in addition to the compact size and robust photostability they are experimentally demonstrated for multiplexed applications such as sensing ions and labeling and imaging for inorganic nanostructures, cells and even nematodes. The converting biomass wastes to promising biocompatible nanomaterials could be a “one-stone-two-birds” strategy to other carbon-containing biomass waste for a highly effectively carbon recycling use and sustainable energy and environment future.

The use of biomass as renewable and sustainable energy source has attracted the attention of politics and research and development (R&D) facilities around the world. Agricultural straw acts as a typical biowaste, which still needs highly effective recycling to save the biomass urgently at present. Photoluminescent carbon dots (C-dots) are novel biocompatible nanomaterials that have been proved to be produced from many carbon-abundant materials and hold great promise for the modern nanobiomedicine. In order to realize a “one-stone-two-birds” strategy, we report a green, economic, one-pot method in this article for synthesizing photoluminescent C-dots by hydrothermal treatment of wheat straw. Using X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) and X-ray diffraction (XRD), we show that the as-prepared C-dots are amorphous in structure and are mainly composed of carbon. Their tiny size (<2nm), combined with the characteristic excitation-dependent relatively bright emission, and robust photostability made the C-dots a potential biocompatible nanomaterial for bio-applications. We have experimentally demonstrated their potential applications in biomedical labeling, imaging, and sensing/detecting. The high yield (∼20%) of C-dots from wheat straw may suggest a new economic strategy for recycling biowaste.

ce:section.ce:para: Agricultural straws, the main crop residues, are one of the most abundantly available and important renewable resources that can be comprehensively used. China is the largest producer of agricultural straws in the world, whose annual total yield is no less than 0.8 billion tons [1]. Approximately 32.3% is used as fuels, approximately 27.1% (such as barley and oat straw) is typically baled and removed from fields for use as roughage in livestock feed rations, and approximately 14.1% is returned to the soil [2]. Renewable energy policies have become embodied legislation in many countries as part of the drive to reduce reliance on fossil fuels [3] and mitigate greenhouse gas emissions [4]. Hence, there is an urgent need to develop a new strategy for effective recycling of these biowastes in order to result in a sustainable improvement of the economy.

Since their discovery by Scrivens and coworkers [5], carbon dots (C-dots) have attracted increasing attention of researchers not only because of their unique combination of bright photoluminescence (PL), high aqueous solubility, easy functionalization, low toxicity, and high photostability [6–8] but also because of their simple synthesis [9] by many approaches from a variety of starting materials, such as simple burning [10] or microwave heating [11] of organic matter [12] and even from natural waste products such as coffee grounds [13] and soy milk [14]. Compared with nanodiamonds, whose emission occurs due to point defects in their sp3-hybridized carbon structures [15], owing to their arrangement of carbon atoms, the light-emitting C-dots share many properties with other electron-rich carbon materials with sp2 hybridization, including photocatalytic action [16].

The simple and inexpensive one-step synthesis [17–19] of C-dots allows the reactive amino groups to present on their surface, in addition to carboxylic groups (which are common). This strongly facilitates their functionalization [20], which further extends the application of C-dots in biological imaging [21], drug delivery [22], sensing [23,24], photovoltaic devices [25,26], lasing [27], and catalysis [16]. Thus, C-dots have become a “new star” member of the carbon family in all scientific researching fields and industries.

Their unique optical characteristics, wide range of applications, and easy synthesis from many carbon-containing substances enable a “one-stone-two-birds” strategy for not only making the best use of agricultural straw but also developing a new facile, green, economic, and one-pot synthesis method for mass yield of photoluminescent C-dots for nano-biomedical applications. In order to implement this strategy, in this article, wheat straw is considered as a model biowaste (which has a lower feed value than barley or oat straw) for high yield of biocompatible C-dots, which are further discussed and experimentally demonstrated for their wide and potential applications in biomedical labeling, imaging, and sensing/detecting.

Poly(sodium 4-styrenesulfonate), poly(allylamine hydrochloride), and quinine sulfate (98%, suitable for fluorescence) were purchased from Sigma–Aldrich (New York, USA). Wheat straw was obtained from local (Inner Mongolia Autonomous Region, Hohhot, People's Republic of China) farmers. All other reagents such as NaOH, FeCl3, CaCl2, Na2CO3, boric acid, NaCl, NaH2PO4, Na2HPO4, tryptone, yeast extract, agar, and the metal-ion compounds were of analytical reagent grade supplied by Sinopharm Chemical Reagent Co. Ltd. (Shanghai, People's Republic of China) and were used as received.

C-dots were prepared by hydrothermal treatment of wheat straw, which was smashed into smaller pieces and washed with water. Typically, 0.2g of the prepared wheat straw sample was added to 10mL of Milli-Q water. The mixture was transferred to a 50-mL steel-lined autoclave and heated at 250°C for 10h. The precipitate was discarded and the supernatant was subjected to dialysis against Milli-Q water through a cellulose ester membrane bag (molecular weight cutoff: 1kDa) to remove small molecules, and then to centrifugation at 10,000rpm for 10min. The pellet was dried under vacuum for 48h, and re-dispersed in Milli-Q water at a concentration of 0.2mg/mL.

The apparent Z-average hydrodynamic diameter (D h) and polydispersity index (PDI) of C-dots (NPs) were measured by dynamic light scattering (DLS) using a Malvern Zetasizer Nano ZS90 (Malvern Instruments Ltd., Worcestershire, UK), equipped with quartz or disposable cuvette (DTS0012, minimum sample volume is 1mL) fitted with a 633-nm He–Ne laser beam. Measurements were taken at 25°C and 90° scattering angle. D h and PDI were obtained by cumulant analysis (software of Zetasizer Nano ZS90, Malvern Instruments Ltd.).

All atomic force microscopy (AFM) images were obtained using a commercial AFM instrument (NanoScope IIId, Bruker Corporation, USA) equipped with a J-scanner (125μm×125μm). The tapping mode in air was used to image the C-dots. Commercial silicon cantilevers with spring constants in the normal range of approximately 48N/m and resonant frequencies of approximately 300kHz (Tap300 AI-G, BudgetSensors) were used for the air tapping mode of imaging. All images were captured at a scan rate of 1–2Hz and a resolution no less than 256 samples×256 lines. AFM images were processed using the flatten function to remove any tilt of images, and analyzed with the Offline software version 5.12 (Digital Instrument).

The absorbance and the PL spectra were recorded on an ultraviolet–visible (UV–vis) spectrometer (U-2900, Hitachi) and a fluorescence spectrometer (Fluorolog®-MAX 4, Horiba), respectively, which were equipped with a 1.0-cm quartz cell. For measuring fluorescence, both excitation and emission slits were set up to 5nm. The quantum yield (φ) of the as-prepared C-dots was determined by a comparative method [28]. A standard solution of quinine sulfate (reported quantum yield: 54%) was prepared by dissolving it in 0.1M H2SO4 (refractive index η =1.33). Aqueous solutions containing varying concentrations of C-dots were prepared by dissolving in Milli-Q water (η =1.33). The values of absorbance of the solutions at the excitation wavelength were recorded using a UV–vis spectrophotometer. PL emission spectra of all the samples were recorded at an excitation wavelength of 350nm. The samples were then analyzed by comparing the integrated PL emission intensity at the excitation wavelength, where the C-dots and the reference showed the same absorbance. The quantum yield was calculated using the following equation:
φ x
=
φ
st
Grad x
Grad
st
η x 2
η
st
2
,
where “st” and “x” denote the standard and test groups, respectively; φ is the fluorescence quantum yield; “Grad” is the gradient obtained from the plot of integrated fluorescence intensity against absorbance; and η is the refractive index of the solvent. In order to minimize the reabsorption effects, absorbance in the 1.0-cm cuvette was not allowed to exceed 0.10 at the excitation wavelength.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Labeling CaCO3 particles and bacteria for imaging
ce:section.ce:para: A small volume of 0.5mL of C-dots (0.2mg/mL) was mixed with 0.5mL of CaCl2 (0.33M) under magnetic stirring at 900rpm. Then, 0.5mL of Na2CO3 (0.33M) was added dropwise to this mixture, and the stirring was continued for 1min. The pellet was washed thrice with Milli-Q water. First, 2mL of C-dots (0.2mg/mL) was added to 100mL of an Luria-Bertani (LB) liquid medium. Escherichia coli TOP10 cells were grown in such an LB liquid medium at 37°C, with stirring at 200rpm for 12h. Only 1mL of the E. coli medium was centrifuged at 10,000rpm for 10min, and the supernatant was trashed and subsequently washed twice with 1×PBS (phosphate-buffered saline) buffer. A drop of this suspension was placed on a glass slide for conducting the imaging experiment, and the images were recorded with an Olympus F-1200 LSCM (laser scanning confocal microscopy).

Fe3+ solutions with different concentrations were prepared by dissolving FeCl3 in Milli-Q water. Fe3+ was detected by dissolving 1mL of C-dots (0.2mg/mL) in 39mL of borax buffer solution (pH 6.0). For the sensing assay, 5μg/mL of C-dots mixed with various concentrations (2.0, 15.6, 31.3, 62.5, 125.0, 250.0μM) of Fe3+ solution to a final volume of 2mL was used to record the PL spectra by fluorescence spectroscopy.

The as-prepared C-dots can be readily dissolved in an aqueous solution, and they were first evaluated by DLS and AFM. An average hydrodynamic size of 1.7±0.23nm with a PDI of 0.422 was obtained from the DLS measurement (Fig. 1A). By contrast, the cross-sectional analysis of the AFM image performed in air revealed a typical topographic height of approximately 1.40nm (Fig. 1C and D), which is a little less than that found by the DLS results, because of the AFM imaging mode in air. The zeta-potential measurement proved that the surface of the as-prepared C-dots is negatively charged (Fig. 1B).

The C-dots dissolved in pure water showed a characteristic absorption spectrum with peaks at 247 and 334nm, and a relatively narrow and symmetric emission spectrum (Fig. 1A). It is similar to most of the reported photoluminescent C-dots [29] and G-dots [30]. The PL of our C-dots is also excitation-dependent (Fig. 1B), which is generally believed to be attributed to the optical selection of differently sized NPs (quantum effect) and different emissive traps on the surface of C-dots [31,32]. Because of their bright blue appearance, the as-prepared C-dots can act as a fluorescent ink as demonstrated in Fig. 2 C and D.

From both the excitation–emission matrices (EEMs, Fig. 3A) and the three-dimensional (3D) PL plot (Fig. 3B) of the diluted aqueous C-dots solution, two PL bands can be distinguished. The strongest emission is centered at 418nm upon 304-nm excitation, and the other local maximum PL centered at 464nm was also observed upon blue-light (364nm) excitation. When the excitation wavelength is increased from 304 to 364nm, the emission decreases in intensity and red-shifts from 418 to 464nm. Considering the obvious absorption bonds and excitation-dependent emissions, we propose that the PL of our C-dots is due to the intrinsic state emission [33]. The quantum yield of the as-prepared C-dots using the comparable approach [28] is determined to be approximately 9.2%, which is considered appreciable for the current one-pot economic, green synthesis using biowaste wheat straw as the raw material.

In order to obtain detailed structural and component information of the as-prepared C-dots, we also measured their X-ray diffraction (XRD) and X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS). The XRD data indicate that our C-dots are amorphous (see Fig. S1), similarly to other reports [34]. From the wide-scan XPS spectrum depicted in Fig. 4 , three strong peaks at 285.0, 400.0, and 532.5eV are attributed to oxygen, nitrogen, and carbon, respectively [35]. Hence, the major elemental components of the as-prepared C-dots are C (81.36%), O (15.74%), and N (1.85%) (see Table S1). Another weak peak at 1071.5eV can be attributed to Na1s, which may arise from the NaCl in the wheat straw [34]. The C1s spectrum (Fig. 4B) shows three peaks at 284.9, 286.0, and 288.9eV, which are attributed to CC, CN, and CN/CO, respectively [36]. The N1s spectrum (Fig. 4C) shows a single peak at 399.5eV, which is attributed to the CNC bond [37]. The O1s spectrum (Fig. 4D) exhibits two peaks at 532.5 and 533.5eV, which are attributed to the CO and COH/COC groups, respectively [38].

The photostability of a fluorophore is a very important parameter that determines the feasibility toward successful bio-applications. From Fig. 5 , it is clear that the PL intensity of C-dots remains relatively constant with ion strength varying up to 1.0M (Fig. 5A), and for a long period, up to 12h of white-light illumination (Fig. 5C), which indicates the robust photostablity of our C-dots in aqueous solution. However, pH tests show that the PL intensities of C-dots decrease with high and low pH values, but they remain relatively constant in the pH range of 5–8. Moreover, obvious photobleaching can also be observed after 5h of continuous UV excitation (Fig. 5D). This is caused by the strong effect of the surrounding factors on the emission control of the surface/molecular state, such as solvent pH [39], which could account for the decrease in the PL intensity of our C-dots at a very low/high pH or on being exposed to high-power UV radiation.

The unique PL characteristics of C-dots have already attracted the interest of many researchers for the labeling and imaging applications [40–43]. In this article, we have demonstrated two typical and potential applications of labeling nanostructures and cells, and their imaging under microscopy.

The capsules made by layer-by-layer (LBL) self-assembly have demonstrated their paramount roles in modern nano-biomedical applications [44–46]. In general, a biocompatible template, such as CaCO3 particles, can control the size of the LBL capsules, co-precipitate the interesting molecules such as drugs and fluorophores, and eventually be encapsulated into the LBL capsules as kinds of fluorescent labels/tags after the CaCO3 templates are dissolved by ethylenediaminetetraacetic acid (EDTA). These labels can be of much interest for in situ tracing of the nanostructures in cells or in vivo studying [46]. However, the toxicity concerns about the fluorophores excluded many bright photoluminescent NPs such as CdSe/ZnS quantum dots (Q-dots) from the biomedical applications [47]. Co-precipitation of C-dots into CaCO3 templates (∼2μm in diameter) has successfully labeled the LBL capsules with the templates (Fig. 6 ). The unique excitation-dependent PL characteristics of C-dots can be responsible for the bright PL in both blue and red channels, which can be in turn very useful to exclude the disturbance from the auto-fluorescence of biomolecules. This could also act as a kind of small molecules that mimic the drug delivery process to optimize the conditions for controlling the pores of the capsules.

In addition to nonliving matter, fluorophores have been widely used in labeling the living. Because of the toxicity concern and its difference from nonliving labeling, the labeling of the living normally requires high-tech processes of greater complexity, which is mainly due to the fact that the labeling must be mild enough to keep the living alive, and most of the chemical conjugating reactions require unfavorable conditions that are very toxic or even lethal to living matter. Nevertheless, it has been discovered that the fluorescent NPs can physically bind to or be taken up by cells as a good label for imaging [48–51]. In practice, we have attempted to label a typical bacterial cell E. coli with our C-dots. It is clear from Fig. 7 that the rod-shaped E. coli can be homogeneously and successfully labeled by C-dots.

In order to make it more interesting, we selected a kind of genetic worm model, named Caenorhabditis elegans, to demonstrate the successful labeling and imaging functionality of our C-dots for their potential applications in biomedical fields. It is clear from Fig. 8 that a sigmoid worm is homogeneously labeled by our C-dots, and both the blue and green PLs can be clearly observed in the LSCM channels. Although the quantum yield of our C-dots is relatively lower than those synthesized using pure chemicals such as citric acid and urea [21] and ethylenediamine [52], the results of labeling both the nonliving and living show that the current quantum yield is satisfactory with LSCM (Figs. 6–8). In additional consideration of the economic, green, and one-pot synthesis, it could be a better way to recycle the biowaste in the early research and development (R&D) stage.

Because of their small size, NP-based fluorophores have a very high surface-to-volume ratio. The smaller the size of the fluorescent NPs, the more the atoms exposed on their surface. Subtle interactions between these surface atoms and the surrounding molecules can significantly influence the PL characteristics of NPs, which is the main reason for their application in bio-sensing and detecting [39,53,54]. It is reported that C-dots did not display any pH dependence in the pH range of 2–8 [55]. However, as shown in Fig. 5B, in the acidic pH range (2–5), the PL intensity of our C-dots is proportional to the pH value; therefore, in principle, it could be used to sense the proton concentration within such a pH range.

In addition, we also demonstrate the ionic-sensing functionality of our C-dots for metal ions, as shown in Fig. 9A, and among 13 kinds of metal ions, Fe3+ shows the highest sensitivity. Because Fe3+ would combine with OH− in alkaline conditions and precipitate, which will not be conducive to the formation of C-dot–Fe3+ aggregates, the PL intensity of the C-dots cannot be well quenched [56]. Therefore, for the Fe3+-sensing test, a partially neutral environment was chosen. As shown in Fig. 9B, when Fe3+ with varying concentrations was added to the 5-μg/mL C-dot solution, the PL intensity of the C-dots was gradually quenched, which results in a strong coordination between Fe3+ ions and the negatively charged moieties on the C-dot surface (as verified by the zeta-potential measurements in Fig. 1B). This interaction would undermine the stability of the PL properties of surface functional groups, leading to the aggregation of C-dots and quenching of the PL, which can be described using the Stern–Volmer equation as follows:
(1)
F 0
F
= 1 +
K
SV
[
Fe
3 +
] ,
where F 0 and F are the PL intensities without and with Fe3+, respectively, and K SV is the Stern–Volmer quenching constant. A typical Stern–Volmer plot is depicted in Fig. 9C, and a good linear fitting function of F 0/F =1.02871+0.00268×[Fe3+] can be obtained over the concentration range of 1.95×10−6–2.5×10−4 M. The binding constant of Fe3+ with C-dots is determined to be 2.68mM−1, with a detection limit of 1.95μM.

Intracellular sensing techniques for different analytes developed rapidly. In addition to the naked C-dots, modified C-dots with specific recognizing ligands can be used for sensing or detecting larger molecules, such as hydrogen sulfide [23], blood sugar [57], dopamine [58], and even cells in aqueous solutions [59,60], which still benefits from their active surface moieties for chemical conjugations. In their recent published work, Weng et al. achieved a detection limit of 10−3 CFUmL−1 of E. coli in tap water, apple juice, and human urine with their mannose-modified C-dots [59]. Very recently, Wang's group developed a Förster resonance energy transfer (FRET)-based method for detecting two kinds of pathogenic bacteria, in which they used green and red semiconductor Q-dots modified with aptamers for recognizing bacteria as the FRET donors and the C-dots as the acceptor [60]. The underlying principle is that the C-dots can effectively quench the PL of Q-dots; therefore, upon addition of the aptamers, the bacteria will replace the C-dots around the Q-dots and therefore recover the PL intensity of the Q-dot.

In this article, we have developed a facile, about 20% high yield, one-step method for the synthesis of photoluminescent C-dots by hydrothermal treatment of wheat straw toward a green, low-cost, and mass yield of the novel promising carbon nanomaterial industry. A comprehensive characterization of the as-prepared C-dots has been achieved by employing DLS, AFM, XRD, XPS, and absorption and fluorescence spectroscopies. We have thus obtained the carbon-dominated, amorphous carbon nanospheres with an average hydrodynamic diameter of 1.7nm, which exhibit robust photostability during testing at high ionic strength, moderate pH, and prolonged light illumination. In order to verify their potential in biomedical applications, we have demonstrated their labeling, imaging, and sensing for nonliving and living matter, respectively. We hope this study could extend the new “one-stone-two-birds” strategy to other carbon-containing biomass waste for a highly effective carbon recycling for use as a sustainable energy resource in the future, especially in developing countries.
The manuscript was written through contributions of all the authors, who have given approval to the final version of the manuscript.

This work was supported by grants from the National Natural Science Foundation of China (Nos. 21171086 and 81160213), Inner Mongolia Grassland Talent (No. 108-108038), Natural Science Foundation of Inner Mongolia Autonomous Region of China (No. 2013MS1121), Inner Mongolia Department of Science and Technology (No. 211-202077), and the Inner Mongolia Agricultural University (Nos. 109-108040, 211-109003 and 211-206038).
The authors declare no competing financial interest.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data associated with this article can be found, in the online version, at http://dx.doi.org/10.1016/j.apsusc.2015.07.095.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: The following are the supplementary data to this article:
